ref.title: The Internal Constitution of the Stars
ref.author: Arthur Eddington

feed_card.headline: The energy in today's sunlight took roughly 170,000 years to escape the Sun, then just eight minutes to reach you.

teasers: Why the eight-minute trip is the easy part
How a gamma ray becomes the light you see
What neutrinos reveal that sunlight cannot

sections.content: The energy in today's sunlight took roughly 170,000 years to escape the Sun, then just eight minutes to reach you.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><circle cx='150' cy='155' r='110' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.45'/><circle cx='150' cy='155' r='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.75'/><path d='M150 155 L168 132 L142 120 L175 105 L150 92 L190 96 L205 120 L185 130 L218 112 L228 77' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><line x1='232' y1='77' x2='340' y2='77' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M340 77 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><circle cx='355' cy='77' r='10' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.8'/><text x='286' y='66' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='14' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='middle'>8 min</text><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle'><text x='150' y='178'>core</text><text x='355' y='103'>Earth</text></g><text x='150' y='240' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='16' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>~170,000 yr</text></svg>

sections.content: Eight minutes is the number everyone remembers, and it makes sunlight feel almost instant, fired across the gap while you were tying your shoes. That part is true. What it hides is everything before it. The energy left the core as a gamma ray and then spent something like 170,000 years trapped inside the Sun, knocked in a new direction every fraction of a centimeter, before it ever reached the surface to start the quick trip out. The hard part of the journey is the part nobody pictures, the 700,000 kilometers of dense plasma between the core and the light of day.

items: The energy in this morning's light broke free in the core about 170,000 years ago, before the last ice age had even begun.
Light crosses the 150 million kilometers of open space to Earth in eight minutes. Getting through the final 700,000 kilometers inside the Sun took that same energy about ten billion times longer.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 85 400 135' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='108' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>When today's sunlight was made</text><line x1='70' y1='160' x2='350' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='70' y1='144' x2='70' y2='178' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='161' y1='150' x2='161' y2='170' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.6' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='350' y1='150' x2='350' y2='170' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.6' stroke-linecap='round'/><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle'><text x='70' y='136' fill='var(--accent)' font-weight='700'>forged</text><text x='161' y='136' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.7'>last ice age</text><text x='350' y='136' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.7'>today</text></g><g font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle'><text x='70' y='198' fill='var(--accent)' font-weight='700'>~170,000 yr ago</text><text x='161' y='198' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.65'>~115,000 yr ago</text><text x='350' y='198' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.65'>8-min trip</text></g></svg>

sections.content: Nobody clocked a photon. The number comes from knowing how far light travels inside the Sun before it strikes something, and how many of those steps it takes to work outward. Deep in the radiative zone, the inner seventy percent of the Sun's radius, the plasma is so dense that a packet of light moves on the order of a millimeter before it is absorbed and thrown back out in a random direction. Follow that drunkard's walk through a real model of the Sun's density and opacity and the climb comes to roughly 170,000 years, the figure Mitalas and Sills calculated in 1992 after showing the usual quick estimate ran far too short. Older textbooks still say a million years, which is why the honest answer is a wide one, tens of thousands to a few hundred thousand depending on the assumptions.

content.body: In the early 1920s no one knew what held a star up or what kept it lit. Arthur Eddington, an English astronomer with a gift for physical reasoning, worked out that a star is a standoff: gravity pulling every layer inward, the pressure of hot gas and trapped radiation pushing back. In his 1926 book The Internal Constitution of the Stars he described the energy inside a star as radiation slowly leaking outward through layer after layer of gas, the picture that still sits under every estimate of how long light takes to get out. He had already argued, back in 1920, that the Sun runs on hydrogen fusing into helium, years before anyone could explain how. The slow-leak idea was right. Nailing the exact timing took decades more and far better models of the solar interior, the work Mitalas and Sills brought to a sharp number in 1992.

key_figures.name: Arthur Eddington
key_figures.role: Built the theory of stellar interiors
key_figures.one_line: His 1926 treatment of a star as radiation slowly leaking outward through hot gas is the foundation under every estimate of how long light takes to escape the Sun.

content.image_caption: The Sun's visible surface, the photosphere, the layer where energy that left the core long ago finally escapes into space.
content.image_attribution: Matúš Motlo, "The Sun in white light", CC BY-SA 4.0 (Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: Neutrinos arrive with the news the light cannot bring
content.body: The same fusion that makes the trapped light also throws off neutrinos, faint particles that barely touch matter at all. A neutrino crosses the entire Sun in about two seconds and reaches Earth eight minutes later, while the light's energy from that same reaction is still tens of thousands of years short of the surface. So when detectors like Borexino caught solar neutrinos in real time in 2014, they were reading the core as it burns right now. The sunlight leaving the surface today set out before the last ice age. We see the Sun's face as ancient history and its heart as live.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 80 400 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='106' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Two messengers, one core</text><text x='18' y='150' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='start'>neutrino</text><line x1='120' y1='150' x2='342' y2='150' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M342 150 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><text x='330' y='141' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'>8 min</text><text x='170' y='169' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle'>~2 s in the Sun</text><text x='18' y='195' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='start'>light's energy</text><path d='M120 195 L132 188 L144 202 L156 188 L168 202 L180 188 L192 202 L204 188 L216 202 L228 188 L240 202 L252 188 L264 202 L276 188 L288 202 L300 188 L312 202 L324 188 L336 202 L350 195' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.55' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><text x='300' y='187' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='end'>~170,000 yr</text></svg>

content.title: The outer third boils the heat to the surface
content.body: The long crawl is only the radiative zone. Above about seventy percent of the way out, the gas can no longer pass the heat along by radiation quickly enough, so it starts to churn, columns of hot plasma rising and cooler plasma sinking back down. Through that convective layer the energy rides the moving gas and crosses the last stretch in weeks rather than millennia. The grainy cells that mottle the Sun's surface in a close photograph are the tops of those churning columns.

sections.content: A star turns out to be a kind of slow memory. The energy in its light was laid down over thousands of years like sediment, and only now reaches the surface. That lag is also a cushion. At any moment the Sun holds a vast reserve of energy in transit, part of why its output stays so steady from one century to the next, with no flicker as the core shifts. The light reaching you is old almost by design, a fire wrapped in so much insulation that the burning and the shining are separated by the whole length of human prehistory.

content.question: Why does the energy in sunlight take so much longer to leave the Sun than to cross space to Earth?
options: Light physically slows down inside the dense solar plasma
It is absorbed and re-emitted endlessly, moving in a random walk
The Sun's gravity bends its path into a long spiral
It waits in the core until enough energy builds up to push it out
content.explanation: Inside the Sun light travels only a tiny distance before being absorbed and thrown out in a random direction, so it zigzags for something like 170,000 years. In open space it runs straight at full speed, covering the distance to Earth in eight minutes.

content.question: A neutrino and a photon's energy are released by the same fusion reaction in the core. Why does the neutrino reach Earth so much sooner?
options: Neutrinos travel faster than the speed of light
Neutrinos are created much closer to the surface
Neutrinos barely interact with matter, so they stream almost straight out
Neutrinos are swept along by the solar wind
content.explanation: A neutrino almost never interacts with anything, so it crosses the Sun in about two seconds and arrives in eight minutes, while the light's energy from the same reaction random-walks outward for tens of thousands of years.

content.question: What becomes of a gamma ray's energy on its long journey out of the Sun?
options: It stays a single gamma ray until it leaves the surface
It instantly converts into a neutrino partway out
It is gradually divided among many lower-energy photons, ending as visible light
It is destroyed and a fresh gamma ray takes its place at the surface
content.explanation: At each collision the energy is shared and degraded, so the single high-energy gamma ray born in the core emerges from the surface as many photons of ordinary visible light.

content.question: Why is it not quite right to say the photon you see is 170,000 years old?
options: The journey really takes only eight minutes
Photons cannot exist inside the Sun at all
The Sun is far older than that anyway
It is the energy, not one particular photon, that makes the long journey
content.explanation: The original photon is absorbed and re-emitted countless times, so what survives is the energy handed along a chain of photons. The light that finally escapes was created at the surface moments before its trip to Earth.

content.question: In which part of the Sun does the energy spend almost all of that long escape time?
options: The dense radiative zone, the inner region
The convective zone, the outer third
The corona, the outer atmosphere
The photosphere, the visible surface
content.explanation: The slow random walk happens in the dense radiative zone. In the outer convective zone, rising and sinking gas carries the energy up far faster, in weeks rather than millennia.

content.question: Detecting solar neutrinos in real time lets scientists do what?
options: Predict solar flares several days in advance
Measure exactly how old the sunlight is
Watch the core's fusion as it happens now
See the surface granules as they form
content.explanation: Because neutrinos leave the core immediately, catching them shows the fusion going on at the center right now, unlike the light, which reports on energy released long ago.

content.myth: A photon from the Sun's core is over a hundred thousand years old by the time it reaches you.
content.reality: The original photon is absorbed and re-emitted countless times. It is the energy that is ancient; the light that escapes the surface was made moments before its eight-minute trip.

content.myth: It takes light a million years to get out of the Sun.
content.reality: That older estimate runs high. Careful models put the figure near 170,000 years, with an honest range from tens of thousands to a few hundred thousand.

content.myth: The eight-minute figure is the whole story of how old sunlight is.
content.reality: Eight minutes is only the trip through empty space. The energy's slow climb through the Sun outweighs it by about ten billion to one.

content.body: The estimate is only as good as the model of the Sun's insides, and the hardest input is opacity, how readily the plasma absorbs light at each depth and temperature. Small revisions to opacity shift the escape time, and a stubborn mismatch between solar models and the sound waves read from the Sun's surface, the solar abundance problem, shows the interior is not fully pinned down. The exact age of sunlight rides on getting that physics right.
items: How much does uncertainty in the plasma's opacity change the true escape time?
Can the clash between solar models and helioseismology, the solar abundance problem, be resolved?

content.label: Sun: Facts. NASA Science (core and surface temperature, layers, energy taking about 170,000 years through the radiative zone)
Mitalas R, Sills KR. On the photon diffusion time scale for the Sun. Astrophysical Journal 1992;401:759
Strobel N. The interior of the Sun and energy transport. Astronomy Notes (random walk of photons, gamma rays degrading to visible light)
How long does light from the Sun take to reach Earth? BBC Sky at Night Magazine (8 minutes 20 seconds across 1 AU)
Borexino Collaboration. Neutrinos from the primary proton-proton fusion process in the Sun. Nature 2014;512:383
Eddington AS. The Internal Constitution of the Stars (1926)
Last Glacial Period. Wikipedia (about 115,000 to 11,700 years ago)
Buldgen G et al. Helioseismic inference of the solar radiative opacity. Nature Communications 2024;15:10261 (the unresolved solar abundance problem)
